  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 up +1.9% (2.1% June 2025 3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (56% actual + 44% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 34)

  • Q2 up +1.30 to 64.97, up +2.0% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. Now that enough companies have reported for Q2, and it currently shows an increase, the rating has changed to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.02 ( looser) to -0.57 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.01 (looser) to -0.52 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex down -0.03 (looser) to -0.55 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three were recently solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -5 to 416, up +15 m/m (232 8/30/24 - 421 tied with 5/16/25) Components: Yield curve 67 Phillips curve 354
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.0785 to -0.7950 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +3.5 to +46.0 as of 7/30/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains near its best post-pandemic readings.

For over a year IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession, although in the last month it has edged closer to their threshold.

Trade weighted US$

  • Up +8.02 to 120-41 w/w, down -2.9% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +1.11 to 98.76 w/w, down -4.3% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are positives.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -2.85 to 100.62 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +6.2% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 139.44, down -18.26 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Down -1.3% YoY.

Beginning three months ago the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. This week the industrial measure turned lower YoY, and so negative. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms, they would not nearly have risen so much YoY.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Down -2.4% to 6238.01.

In early April stocks made a 12 month low, but this was more than 3 months ago. This month, several times this week, they have made several new all-time highs. As a result this indicator changed from neutral to positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State, up +16.2 to 2.0
  • Philly up +16.1 to 18.4
  • Richmond down -13 to -25
  • Kansas City up +13 to +2
  • *Dallas up +3.7 to -3.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +1 to -1.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year, but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then one month+ ago to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempting to front-run new tariffs - which would account to the sharp reversals beginning in March.

In April, I wrote that

“I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.”

Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State up +3.9 to -9.3
  • Philly up +1.0 to -16.3
  • Richmond up +6 to -8
  • Kansas City down -5 to -2
  • *Dallas up +6.4 to +2.0
  • Average: up +1 to -7.

Contra the above non-manufacturing reports, the ISM non-manufacturing report turned more positive in April. It has stabilized at negative levels since May.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 218,000, up +1,000 w/w, down -12.1% YoY
  • 4-week average 221,000, down -3,500 w/w, down -6.6% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. We have had very good reports for the last five weeks, and last week this indicator has improved to positive.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up +1 to 85 w/w
  • Down -2.7% YoY (low -13.6%- high -4.7% 7/18/25).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $245.5 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $228.8 B last year, up +$16.7 B or +7.3%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$2.25 to $67.31 w/w, down -6.0% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices unchanged at $3.12 w/w, down -$0.36 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -3.0% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased, but have since receded back to their more normal recent range.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.39% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), up +0.09% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record has to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • June 2025 vs. June 2024: $28.0 B vs. $7.6 B (+$20.6 B)
  • July 1-30 2025 vs. July 1-16 2024: $29.0 B vs. $8.6 B (+20.4 B).

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.31 to 2.56 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and came very close to setting a new 12 month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in neutral range, including a new 6 month low, six weeks ago it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • Last week - 7 day average up +13% YoY, vs. +10% one week prior.

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general this year the YoY trends have improved, but with a great deal of noise.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +4.9% YoY, 4 week average +5.2% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Since then it had been weakening - until three weeks ago, when it apparently stabilized.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Down -0.35% to +1.67% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high nine weeks ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.2% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7%% 12/16/24).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12 month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12 month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12 month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator has weakened, although it is still positive. It did improve along with nominal consumer spending in the past month.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.7%)
  • Intermodal units up +1.3% YoY (cumulative up +4.8%)
  • Total loads up +1.1% YoY (cumulative up +3.8%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +1 to 2184 (new 24 month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -117 to 2003.

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply so far this month, and remained positive this week.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded all the way to the top of its six month range earlier this year, before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.5% w/w
  • Up +4.8% YoY.

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and two weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10 year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr. - 3 mo. Treasury X ✓
2 yr - Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 5 3 6
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities x ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 8 4 3
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 9 1 0

.

There has been very little change in the overall picture of the data in a number of weeks: long leading indicators are very mixed, short leading indicators positive, and coincident indicators very positive.

Probably the most positive are the stock market and initial jobless claims among the short leading indicators. Until at least one of these weakens, it is hard to see the near term picture being negative. And among the coincident indicators, the two that measure consumer behavior - retail spending and restaurant dining - continue quite positive. Until consumers show signs of pulling back, the coincident picture is likely to be good.

That being said, last week I wrote that “This coming week I will be especially watching to see if consumer spending on goods shows signs of further slowing compared with March and April; and also whether there is payback from last month’s big upward seasonal adjustment in education hiring, which was nearly half of the entire employment gain.” Indeed real spending on goods remained weak compared with March and April, and the June education hiring was completely revised away. The monthly data, as opposed to this weekly data, is at very least flashing yellow, and within the next week could be flashing red, i.e., “recession watch.”

In which regard, the news that broke late Friday that Trump fired the BLS Administrator in response to the weak employment report has so far (as I write this report) been treated as appalling by the financial press. If the reaction to bad news is to immediately fire the messenger, nobody will be able to trust any good news delivered under the new regime. It is fortunate, therefore, that much of the data you see in the above report is generated by private sources or by the Fed, and in at least one case, the States. It appears that cross-checking BLS data with other, non-government sources is likely to become very important starting almost immediately.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday suffered its work week since late May, as market participants that had driven stocks to record highs faced a reality check in a combination of trade developments, weak economic data, and concerns over the independence of federal organizations from U.S. President Donald Trump.

The roller-coaster ride for equities began with the conclusion of the Federal Reserve's fifth monetary policy committee meeting on Wednesday. The central bank held its key rate steady, and chair Jerome Powell signaled that policymakers were in no hurry to cut interest rates. Stocks sold off after the Fed's messaging, and traders were quick to dial back rate cut odds for September.

Those odds took another hit on Thursday, and stocks took an additional beating after the release of a hot inflation indicator. The core personal consumption expenditures price index - widely seen as the Fed's preferred inflation gauge - came in a tad hotter than anticipated on a Y/Y basis for June, and the +2.8% reading remains stubbornly higher than the central bank's desired +2% target.

Then came Friday's nonfarm payrolls report. According to government data, U.S. job growth in July was much weaker than anticipated, while payrolls for May and June were collectively revised lower by 258K. Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US LLP, dubbed it the "worst major economic report in the post-pandemic era." Rate cut odds for September sharply whipsawed back up to above 80% following the data.

Trump alleged that the jobs data had been "rigged" to make him and the Republican party "look bad." He also said he would fire Bureau of Labor Statistics head Erika McEntarfer after accusing her of faking jobs data before the U.S. election last year.

Turning to trade, this week also marked the reinstatement of reciprocal tariffs on several U.S. trading partners that had been postponed twice since their initial announcement on "Liberation Day" in April.

Amid all the week's headlines, investors kept an eye on the second quarter earnings season as well, which picked up significant steam. Grabbing the spotlight were a mixed bag of quarterly reports from tech titans: Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) impressed with their performance, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) disappointed, and Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) largely delivered.
